preFix.title: Hooked rug: floral bouquet pattern
preFix.subtitle: A 40 × 60 cm hand-hooked floral rug on monk's cloth, worked in 6 mm wool strips
preFix.excerpt: A hand-hooked floral rug, 40 × 60 cm, designed on monk's cloth backing. Six large stylised flower heads form a bouquet arrangement on a plain ground. Wool strips approximately 6 mm wide are cut from recycled wool fabric or dyed wool yardage and pulled through the backing with a hook. Covers design transfer, strip cutting, hooking order, and edge binding.
preFix.sourceNotes: Observation-led. Hand-hooking on monk's cloth or hessian backing is a British and North American rug-making tradition with roots in 19th-century domestic craft. The basic hook-and-pull technique and binding method are standard practice across the guild tradition. No modern named author's prose is quoted.

content.text: This hand-hooked rug is worked on
monk's cloth
stretched on a rug frame. Six stylised flower heads are arranged in a bouquet on a plain ground, worked in
wool strips
approximately 6 mm wide. The
pile height
is a consistent 7 mm throughout. Finished size: 40 × 60 cm before binding.
What you need

items.name: Monk's cloth backing, cut to 50 × 70 cm (allows 5 cm border all around for mounting on frame)
Wool fabric or dyed wool yardage, medium weight, in 6 colours for flowers plus 1 colour for ground
Rug hook (medium size, for 6 mm strips)
Rug binding tape or cotton twill tape for edge binding
Sharp scissors or mechanical strip cutter (optional)
Fabric marker or water-soluble pen for design transfer

content.text: Method
Step 1: Transfer the design

Draw the bouquet design onto the monk's cloth with a fabric marker before mounting on the frame. Mark the outer rug border (40 × 60 cm rectangle), then place the six flower heads within it: three flowers in the upper half and three in the lower half, with the stems gathering at the centre base. Each flower head can be a simple circle 8 to 10 cm in diameter, with petals suggested by radiating oval shapes. Mark the stem and leaf lines freehand. Keep the design simple: large shapes hook faster and read more clearly from a distance than small intricate ones.
Step 2: Mount the backing on the frame

Stretch the monk's cloth tightly and evenly on the rug frame, stapling or lacing the border allowance to the frame sides. The cloth must be taut: monk's cloth stretches easily under the tension of hooking, and a slack backing will cause the finished rug to pucker. Check tension in both directions before starting.
Step 3: Cut the wool strips

Cut the wool fabric into strips approximately 6 mm wide along the fabric grain. Straight-grain strips are more stable than bias-cut strips and give a more even pile. A mechanical strip cutter set to size 8 (6 mm) produces consistent strips quickly. By hand, fold the fabric and cut multiple strips at once with sharp scissors, checking width against a ruler. Cut strips approximately 15 to 20 cm long; shorter strips waste less and are easier to manage through the backing.
Step 4: Hook the flower outlines first

Hook the outlines of each flower head before filling. To hook a strip: push the hook through the backing from the top, catch the strip from below, and pull it up through the backing to form a loop. The loop should stand approximately 7 mm above the surface. Continue, inserting the hook into each successive hole in the backing weave, pulling the strip up in loops. At the start and end of each strip, pull the strip end up to the surface rather than leaving it on the back; trim the end flush with the pile.
Step 5: Fill the flower heads

Fill each flower head section with its colour, working in curved rows that follow the outline rather than straight horizontal lines: this gives a more organic, petal-like texture. Fill the petals before the centre circle of each flower. The centre can be a contrasting colour in a small tight spiral of hooking. Stems and leaves are hooked in narrow lines following the marked curves.
Step 6: Fill the ground

Fill the background ground colour last, working in horizontal rows across the full width of the rug. Hook right up to the edges of each flower motif, leaving no unhook backing visible between the motifs and the ground. The ground colour defines the flowers by contrast.
Step 7: Bind the edges

Remove the finished rug from the frame. Trim the backing border to 2 cm all around. Fold the border to the back and press. Stitch rug binding tape or cotton twill tape over the folded edge on the back, mitreing the corners. The binding secures the backing and protects the edges from fraying. Machine stitch or hand stitch using a strong thread.
Common problems

items.fix: Re-stretch the backing if possible. Avoid pulling the strip ends tight: the loops should stand freely at 7 mm without tension. If the rug has already distorted, block it by wetting it thoroughly and pinning it to a flat board at the correct dimensions until dry.
items.cause: Backing was not stretched tightly enough on the frame, or the strips are being pulled too tight as they loop up
items.problem: Rug is puckering or pulling out of shape as hooking progresses

items.fix: Ensure each loop is pulled to at least 6 mm height. Use strips no narrower than 5 mm. If the backing weave is too open, try skipping alternate holes: every second hole gives more grip per loop.
items.cause: Pile height is too low or the strips are too narrow, and the loops are not gripping the backing weave
items.problem: Loops are pulling back through to the underside when the rug is handled

items.fix: Hook the last row of ground right up to the outline of each flower, following the flower shape rather than the straight horizontal pattern. Outline the flower in its own colour first, then fill outward to the ground.
items.cause: Hooking rows in the ground ran parallel to the flower edge rather than abutting it cleanly
items.problem: Colour boundaries between flowers and ground look ragged

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
